## VI. What This Map Is Good For

::: aside
**META:** Closing section. Brief. Don't re-summarize. Three things: (1) what the map can and can't do, (2) what it suggests for next steps, (3) honest statement of what we'd want to see more of. No new content — this is a landing, not a new argument.
:::

This framework is useful for a specific thing: locating where genuine disagreements are. If you come away knowing that Christiano and Yudkowsky aren't disagreeing about how bad a misaligned superintelligence would be (both think: very bad) but about whether current AI behavior is evidence about that risk and whether warning shots are possible — then the map worked. If you can see that Bengio and Yudkowsky share a threat model almost entirely and diverge only on whether architectural choices can prevent the threat from materializing — the map worked. Surfacing the crux is not the same as resolving it, but it's a necessary precondition.

What the map is not useful for: telling you who's right. That's a different project, and this post deliberately doesn't attempt it. Some of the cruxes (especially A and C) may be resolvable by future evidence; others (especially D at long time horizons) are prior-dependent enough that the debate may not converge regardless of what happens. The epistemological stance axis (Cut 2) exists precisely to name this: some of these conversations are structurally unresolvable under current conditions, not because anyone is being unreasonable, but because they're not using the same evidentiary standard.

What this points toward: a crux questionnaire that lets readers self-locate using the same six-crux framework as §V; a probability tree that captures multipolar failure as a genuinely distinct branch rather than a weak version of unipolar failure; and more careful treatment — in this post and in the field — of what endpoint is actually being assigned probability to when someone states a p(doom) number.

---

## Appendix / Notes

::: aside
**META:** Structural scratchpad — open questions, design decisions under consideration, things to check before publishing. Items here are not blocking; they're reminders.
:::

- *[Consider: comparison heatmap across archetypes × cruxes — visual version of the crux table in §IV]*
- *[Pressure-test: are all seven archetypes genuinely distinct? Particular question: is Conditional Doomer doing different work than Structural Doomer enough to warrant two portraits?]*

---

## Sources

::: aside
**META:** Organized by archetype. When adding inline citations to prose, add the source here too. Items marked `[CITATION: ...]` need a URL that hasn't been confirmed yet. Verify all links before publish.
:::

**#1 — Hard Doomer**

- Yudkowsky, ["AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities"](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/uMQ3cqWDPHhjtiesc/agi-ruin-a-list-of-lethalities) (LessWrong, Apr 2022)
- Yudkowsky, ["Pausing AI Developments Isn't Enough. We Need to Shut It All Down"](https://time.com/6266923/ai-pause-dangerous-letter-response/) (TIME, Mar 2023)
- MIRI, ["2024 Mission and Strategy Update"](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/xofpLpFjkMFgYX6JC/miri-2024-mission-and-strategy-update) (LessWrong, Jan 2024)

**#2 — Structural Doomer**

- Bostrom, *[Superintelligence](https://global.oup.com/academic/product/superintelligence-9780198739838)* (Oxford, 2014)
- Bostrom, *[Deep Utopia](https://www.amazon.com/Deep-Utopia-Meaning-Solved-World/dp/1646871952)* (2024) — position shift
- Carlsmith, [*Is Power-Seeking AI an Existential Risk?*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13353) (arXiv, 2022)

**#3 — Conditional Doomer / Risk Realist**

- Christiano, ["My views on 'doom'"](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/a392MCzsGXAZP88Sq/my-views-on-doom) (Alignment Forum, Apr 2023)
- Amodei, 25% p(doom) — [Axios interview](https://www.axios.com/2025/10/01/dario-amodei-anthropic-pdoom-25-percent) (Oct 2025)

**#4 — Multipolar / Governance Realist**

- Critch & Krueger, [ARCHES](https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.04948) (arXiv, 2020)
- Toner, [Georgetown CSET](https://cset.georgetown.edu)
- Russell, *[Human Compatible](https://www.amazon.com/Human-Compatible-Artificial-Intelligence-Problem/dp/0525558616)* (Viking, 2019)
- Ord, *[The Precipice](https://theprecipice.com/)* (Hachette, 2020)
- [CITATION: Critch YouTube Nov 2024 p(doom) video]

**#5 — Gradualist Forecaster**

- Grace et al., ["Thousands of AI Authors on the Future of AI"](https://aiimpacts.org/2023-expert-survey-on-progress-in-ai/) (AI Impacts, Jan 2024)

**#6 — Safe-by-Design Alarmist**

- Bengio et al., ["Managing Extreme AI Risks Amid Rapid Progress"](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adn0117) (*Science*, May 2024)
- [International AI Safety Report](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/international-ai-safety-report-2025) (Jan 2025)
- Russell & Norvig, [CIRL](https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.03137) (arXiv, 2016)
- Russell, *[Human Compatible](https://www.amazon.com/Human-Compatible-Artificial-Intelligence-Problem/dp/0525558616)* (Viking, 2019)

**#7 — Capability / Paradigm Skeptic**

- LeCun, ["A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence"](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=BZ5a1r-kVsf) (2022)
- LeCun, ["AI isn't even as smart as dogs or cats"](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-yann-lecun-ai-not-as-smart-as-dogs-cats-2023-6) (Business Insider, Jun 2023)
- Chollet, [ARC Prize / ARC-AGI](https://arcprize.org)
